title: Calcium oxalate stone: normocalcemic hypercalciuria
vignette: A 32-year-old man comes to the emergency department due to sudden onset of severe right flank pain that radiates toward the groin. He also has gross hematuria but no fever or dysuria. The patient has no significant medical conditions and has never experienced similar symptoms. He takes no medications. Temperature is 36.7 C (98.1 F), blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg, and pulse is 88/min. The right flank is tender to palpation. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Imaging shows a stone in the middle of the right ureter. Which of the following is most likely to be seen on laboratory evaluation of this patient?

clues.text: severe right flank pain that radiates toward the groin
clues.note: Colicky flank-to-groin pain is classic renal colic from a ureteral stone. The location and radiation track the stone moving down the ureter.

clues.text: gross hematuria but no fever or dysuria
clues.note: Hematuria without infection signs fits nephrolithiasis. Absence of fever/dysuria argues against UTI/pyelonephritis as the cause.

clues.text: a stone in the middle of the right ureter
clues.note: Imaging confirms a ureteral calculus. Most stones are calcium (oxalate > phosphate), and the leading metabolic driver is hypercalciuria.

whatMatters: This is a calcium (oxalate) kidney stone in a young, otherwise healthy man. The most common metabolic abnormality is idiopathic hypercalciuria WITH a normal serum calcium — so labs show normocalcemia plus hypercalciuria.
strategy: For 'most common stone / lab finding' questions, default to calcium oxalate stones driven by hypercalciuria, and remember most hypercalciuria is idiopathic with NORMAL serum calcium (regulated by PTH/vitamin D). Only invoke hypercalcemia if the stem hints at hyperparathyroidism, sarcoid, or malignancy.

eliminations.label: hypercalcemia — implies a specific cause not present
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: hypercalcemia WITH hypercalciuria occurs in primary hyperparathyroidism, sarcoidosis, or malignancy. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: this young, healthy man has no features of those; most stone-formers have idiopathic hypercalciuria with NORMAL serum calcium. ✅ Correct if: the stem hinted at 'stones, bones, groans' / elevated PTH or granulomatous disease.

eliminations.label: uric acid stone — wrong metabolic setup
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: hyperuricemia + hyperuricosuria predisposes to uric acid stones (gout, tumor lysis, high purine turnover). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: nothing here suggests gout/high cell turnover; the default stone is calcium oxalate, not uric acid. ✅ Correct if: the patient had gout, tumor lysis, or acidic urine with radiolucent stones.

eliminations.label: hyperoxaluria — a less common driver
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: hyperoxaluria (high dietary oxalate, fat malabsorption/Crohn, low-calcium diet) can also cause calcium oxalate stones. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it's a LESS common contributor than hypercalciuria, which is the single most common risk factor; no malabsorption clue is given. ✅ Correct if: the stem mentioned Crohn/short-gut, steatorrhea, or a very high-oxalate diet.

eliminations.label: isolated hyperuricosuria — still a uric-acid pathway
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: high urine uric acid with normal serum (e.g., high-purine diet) can promote uric acid or act as a nidus for calcium stones. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it's not the most likely finding for a typical calcium stone; hypercalciuria is. ✅ Correct if: the question specifically built a uric-acid-stone or nidus scenario.

reasoning: Renal colic + hematuria + ureteral stone = nephrolithiasis
The leading risk factor is hypercalciuria, usually idiopathic
Serum calcium stays normal (PTH/vitamin D regulation) → normocalcemia + hypercalciuria → answer C
mechanism: Calcium stones (oxalate > phosphate) are the most common renal calculi, and idiopathic hypercalciuria is the leading risk factor. Excess urinary calcium arises from increased gut absorption, increased bone resorption, or decreased renal tubular reabsorption, while plasma calcium stays normal because PTH and vitamin D keep it regulated. Without an underlying disorder like hyperparathyroidism, patients remain normocalcemic despite spilling calcium into the urine.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Renal > kidney stones table: calcium oxalate (most common; hypercalciuria, often normocalcemic; also hyperoxaluria, hypocitraturia), uric acid (low pH, radiolucent), struvite (urease+ infection), cystine. Memorize the precipitating conditions.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Translate 'most likely lab' into 'most common stone → most common metabolic driver.' Calcium oxalate → hypercalciuria, and emphasize the counterintuitive NORMAL serum calcium.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on hypercalciuria vs hypercalcemia, hyperoxaluria, hyperuricosuria, renal colic, costovertebral angle? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Hypercalcemia+hypercalciuria looks 'more complete' but implies hyperparathyroidism/sarcoid not present here; hyperoxaluria and uric-acid options are real but less common. Default to normocalcemic hypercalciuria unless a special cause is stated.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: Stone imaging matters elsewhere: calcium and struvite stones are radiopaque on X-ray/CT; uric acid stones are radiolucent (seen on CT, not plain film); cystine are faintly opaque. Tie stone type to its imaging in the table.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Tell me what happened (timing, second-guessing, careless click) and I'll suggest a fix for future questions.

keywords.front: Most common risk factor for calcium stones

keywords.back: High-oxalate diet, fat malabsorption/Crohn, low-calcium diet
Urease+ organisms (Proteus), staghorn calculi, alkaline urine
Flank pain radiating to groin + hematuria

firstAid.detail: Renal > kidney stones: calcium oxalate (hypercalciuria — often idiopathic and normocalcemic; hyperoxaluria; hypocitraturia), imaging (radiopaque), prevention.
firstAid.location: Renal > kidney stones: calcium oxalate (hypercalciuria — often idiopathic and normocalcemic; hyperoxaluria; hypocitraturia), imaging (radiopaque), prevention.

firstAid.detail: Renal > uric acid (radiolucent, low pH), struvite (urease/Proteus, staghorn), cystine (cystinuria); their lab and urine-pH associations.
firstAid.location: Renal > uric acid (radiolucent, low pH), struvite (urease/Proteus, staghorn), cystine (cystinuria); their lab and urine-pH associations.

firstAid.detail: Endocrine/Renal > PTH and vitamin D keep serum calcium normal — why most hypercalciuric stone-formers are normocalcemic (vs primary hyperparathyroidism).
firstAid.location: Endocrine/Renal > PTH and vitamin D keep serum calcium normal — why most hypercalciuric stone-formers are normocalcemic (vs primary hyperparathyroidism).

memoryHook: Common stone = calcium oxalate; the urine spills calcium (hypercalciuria) but the blood stays normal — normocalcemic, because PTH keeps the books balanced.
